entries.title: Why My Hands?
entries.story:
Another night under the unholy neon of Broadway in Nashville, Tennessee. Every bar bled its own song into the street, until the mix of guitars and drunken voices became one indistinguishable roar. A thin coating of sweat covered my entire body as my lungs inhaled the moisture in the air. I wasn’t sure exactly what I was looking to shoot, but I knew from experience that Broadway is not short on action.

 After wandering my way between each intersection for over an hour, I could tell I was being carefully watched. After scanning the scene, I noticed an older gentleman sitting on the ledge of “Kid Rock's Big Honky Tonk” behind the bouncer. I acknowledged him and walked closer. I saw he was holding a beer bottle with his withered hands. I was not about to miss such a simple shot. 

As we shook hands, I introduced myself as a photographer and mentioned I wanted to take a picture. He was very cautious about my motives, not letting his guard down. “Well, let me ask you this: why my hands?” he said in a curious tone. I took a second to think. I had to dig deep for a good answer. “We use our hands for so much, and they don’t get enough credit. Hands connect us, divide us, restore us, and kill us. The surgeons use theirs to heal, while the soldiers use theirs to kill. Sculptors use theirs to destroy and create. That’s the contradiction I am trying to show.” He was hooked. Without hesitation, he agreed. That is how the first shot of the beer bottle came to be. Afterward, I asked if I could sit and chat. Before I could get the full sentence out, he scooted over and patted the slim grey wooden ledge. It was more than a simple gesture; it was an invitation into his home. 

His name is Mike; he’s 69 years old, and he has been homeless for 10 years. He moved to Nashville in 1993 looking to escape the redundancy of life in West Tennessee. After having several heart attacks, he had to quit his line of work (which he never mentioned). He had three stents put into his heart, a financial burden he was not prepared for. Welfare that came from the United States Government helped him get by month to month. Ten years ago, his welfare stopped coming, meaning he had no income. He resorted to panhandling at night to get by. No sign, just asking.

After sharing tears, laughs, and talking about life for nearly half an hour, our conversation soon had to end. You can just tell when something intrudes on your time, and for us, it was Mike's bladder. By the time we were done, he finished the rest of his half-empty IPA he had found abandoned earlier that night. Mike stood up, shook my hand, and said, “Well, I’ve got to find a place to piss.” Before he could scatter away, I asked him for a portrait to remember our time spent together. “Sure, why not,” he said. A face with no expression told a whole story on its own.
entries.teaser: "Hands connect us, divide us, restore us, and kill us. The surgeons use theirs to heal, while the soldiers use theirs to kill. Sculptors use theirs to destroy and create."
